title: Safe SHORES Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Safe SHORES Act of 2025 gives money to help people who are recovering from drug addiction find safe places to live. The bill sets aside at least fifty million dollars each year from 2026 to 2030 for this program. States can use this money to help people get housing and even buy furniture for their homes. The government will write reports every year to show how the money was spent and how many people were helped.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Safe and Secure Housing for Opioid Recovery and Enduring Stability Act of twenty twenty five or the Safe SHORES Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Reauthorization of Pilot Program to Help Individuals in Recovery from a Substance Abuse Disorder Become Stably Housed
sections.text: This section updates an existing program that helps people recovering from drug addiction find stable housing. The changes include setting aside at least fifty million dollars each year from twenty twenty six through twenty thirty for this program. States that get money from this program must spend all of it within five years instead of spending thirty percent within one year. States can now use up to one percent of their funds to buy furniture for temporary housing. The program will track grants through a government computer system called the Integrated Disbursement and Information System. States are encouraged to help organizations meet project deadlines, give priority to facilities that offer job training and money management classes, follow up with former residents, meet state standards, and find additional funding sources. The government must write a report every year starting one year after this law passes. The report must include how the money was used, updates on projects, statistics about residents served, successful strategies, and how different government departments can work together to build more recovery housing.
